cannibalization_check.rationale: Rolling-windows targets the tactical 'how the system works' query. Reservation-economy is an ecosystem overview. Different primary keywords, different word-count targets, minimal SERP overlap (1-2 shared domains).

Both have historical narrative framing. Shadow-market targets 'reservation scalping NYC' + legal angle. Who-gets-the-table targets 'history of NYC restaurant access.' Shared queries around 'Rao's history' and 'Le Cirque reservations' could draw on same SERP. Differentiate via distinct primary keywords and clear internal link hierarchy where who-gets-the-table links to shadow-market as the modern follow-on.

cannibalization_check.post_a: Proposed: Resy Drop Times Master List
cannibalization_check.rationale: How-it-works page explains the system at a high level. Drop times list is a data-first resource. Risk if how-it-works page targets 'what time do NYC restaurants release reservations.' Recommended fix: canonically separate these by giving how-it-works the system-explanation role and the drop-times post the data/reference role, with explicit internal link between them.

cannibalization_check.post_a: Proposed: How to Get a Reservation at [Restaurant]
cannibalization_check.rationale: Each restaurant slug page already targets 'Carbone reservation', 'Lilia reservation' etc. A standalone blog post for individual restaurants would directly compete. Resolution: do NOT publish individual restaurant how-to posts — instead, publish a cluster-level post (e.g., 'How to Get a Table at the 10 Hardest Restaurants in NYC') that links to individual slug pages as the canonical data source. This makes slug pages the spoke and the cluster post the hub.

pillar.title: NYC Restaurant Reservation Drop Times: Complete 2026 Guide
secondary_keywords: what time do NYC restaurants release reservations
when does Resy drop reservations
NYC restaurant reservation drop time list
when does Carbone drop on Resy
when does Lilia release reservations
pillar.rationale: NYC RSVPs owns this query with a simple calculator page. Scoopd has the actual restaurant-level data across 192 restaurant slug pages and is the natural publisher of a structured drop-times reference. This is the highest-ROI gap in the current content set because Scoopd already has the underlying data that no competitor has in comparable depth.

pillar.rationale: Bloomberg, CNBC, and reservationfinder.io published platform war coverage in 2026. Scoopd's angle is diner-side, not restaurant-side: which platform holds the tables you want and how does each behave differently for the person trying to book. The /platform/[name] pages become spokes that this pillar links to.

spokes.rationale: Amex/Resy relationship is a high-interest topic that surfaces in every hard-reservation discussion. No current Scoopd post addresses it. Directly relevant to premium users making card decisions. Strong cross-link target from reservation-shadow-market (already references the two-tier system extensively).

pillar.title: How to Get a Reservation at Any Hard Restaurant in NYC
pillar.primary_keyword: how to get a reservation in NYC
secondary_keywords: NYC restaurant reservations tips
NYC restaurant reservation guide 2026
how to book NYC restaurant without Resy
pillar.rationale: This is the highest search volume cluster pillar and the clearest entry point for new organic users. The Infatuation owns this SERP today. Scoopd's differentiation: data-backed tactics (specific drop times, window lengths, walk-in policies) pulled from its own restaurant database rather than anecdote. Every tactic section links to relevant restaurant slug pages and to the drop-times reference post.

spokes.title: Walk-In Guide: Which NYC Restaurants Have Bar Seats and When to Arrive
secondary_keywords: NYC restaurant bar seats walk-in guide
same day restaurant cancellations NYC strategy
spokes.rationale: Walk-in is the fallback strategy for every reader who missed a drop. Timeout has a 'best restaurants you can walk into' article; Scoopd's version is differentiated by covering the walk-in policy specifically at hard-to-book restaurants (Lilia bar, Torrisi bar, Semma barstools) rather than generic easy restaurants.

pillar.rationale: West Village contains the highest density of hard-to-book restaurants on the site (Via Carota, Lilia, Don Angie, Minetta Tavern, 4 Charles, Double Chicken Please, Jeju Noodle Bar). A neighborhood guide acts as a cluster hub linking to all those slug pages while also targeting neighborhood-level search queries. The /neighborhood/west-village page can serve as the category hub and this post as the editorial layer above it.

restaurant_page_links.note: Each blog post in C1 and C3 should inline-link to relevant /restaurant/[slug] pages. Minimum 3 slug links per post. Hardest-restaurants post should link to carbone, lilia, via-carota, torrisi, 4-charles-prime-rib, tatiana, corner-store, the-86, oresh, polo-bar slugs. Walk-in post should link to lilia, torrisi, semma, minetta-tavern, via-carota slugs.
restaurant_page_links.reciprocal: Each linked restaurant slug page should contain a contextual paragraph or callout linking back to the blog post. This is an internal link gap: current slug pages do not link to blog content.

validation.no_orphan_pages: West Village guide and Resy vs OpenTable pillar both need 3+ inbound links; recommend cross-linking from existing shadow-market and rolling-windows posts once new pillars are published
validation.serp_overlap_supports_groupings: All spoke-to-cluster assignments have overlap score >= 4; all same-post merges have score >= 7
